abstract.para: This document describes what &suseconnect; is and how the &scc; (SCC) and Repository Mirroring Tool (RMT) use it to gather updated information about active systems and their hardware environment.

info.meta: How to use &suseconnect; to monitor active systems

revdescription.para: Added information about collector configuration and opt-in/opt-out capabilities for optional collectors. Updated configuration format documentation to reflect the transition from line-by-line format to YAML-based configuration.
Added 'ha_active' to the table of collected system attributes
Added 'kubernetes_provider' to the table of collected system attributes
Added 'Vendor' to the table of collected system attributes
Added pci profile collection information (pci_data, mod_list).

sect1.title: What is &suseconnect;?
sect1.para: &suseconnect; is a tool to register SLE-based operating systems with SCC or &rmt;. Registered systems receive updates to installed packages, for example, to increase security of the operating system. With &suseconnect;, you can additionally register extensions and modules that extend the base system functionality.

tip.para: This document does not cover general usage of &suseconnect;. To find more basic information about how to register your system, modules and extensions with &suseconnect;, refer to .

sect1.para: &suse; is committed to helping provide better insights into the consumption of SUSE subscriptions, regardless of where they are running or how they are managed; physical or virtual, on-premises or in the cloud, connected to SCC or RMT, or managed by &smlm;. To help you identify or filter out systems in SCC that are decommissioned or no longer running, &suseconnect; now features a daily ping, which updates system information automatically. Each registered host contacts SCC or RMT and sends the unique identification of the host and the description of its hardware environment.

sect1.title: Requirements
sect1.para: To use the extended &suseconnect; functionality, you need to:

listitem.para: Run a supported and registered SLE-based host with all update patches applied.
Verify that the host system includes the &suseconnect; command version 0.3.33 or higher.

sect1.title: How enhanced system visibility works
sect1.para: We extended &suseconnect; with the new option --keepalive. The command &suseconnect; --keepalive updates the last time that a host contacted SCC or &rmt; and updates the host's hardware information.
The &suseconnect; package ships with two &systemd; units:

listitem.para: A service which runs the command &suseconnect; --keepalive on demand.
A timer which runs the service suseconnect-keepalive.service once a day at random time to prevent SCC congestion.

sect1.para: These units are responsible for keeping the system information up to date with the SCC or &rmt;, and for providing accurate data about subscription usage.

note.title: The timer is enabled automatically
note.para: When the &suseconnect; package is installed or updated, and its version is equal to or greater than the one described above, the keep-alive timer is enabled automatically.

sect2.title: How do I disable the &suseconnect; keep-alive timer?
sect2.para: If you prefer to not have the &suseconnect; keep-alive timer running on your system, you can disable it with systemctl:
Once the timer is disabled, subsequent updates to the &suseconnect; package do not re-enable it.

tip.title: Re-enabling the keep-alive timer
tip.para: If you decide to re-enable the disabled keep-alive timer, run the following command:

sect1.title: Identifying inactive systems
sect1.para: When &suseconnect; reports details about active hosts daily, SCC or &rmt; collect this information and let you filter out registered inactive hosts. If your hosts are registered against &rmt; or &smlm;, these registration servers forward the received information to SCC.

sect2.title: How to identify inactive systems in SCC?
sect2.para: In the SCC Web user interface, you can limit the list of systems to see only inactive hosts.

step.para: Log in to SCC at and select your organization in the left pane.
Select Systems from the top menu to list all registered hosts by default.
Select Inactive from the upper-left drop-down list.

figure.title: Drop-down list with a filter for inactive hosts

textobject.phrase: A screenshot showing a filter for inactive hosts

step.para: If you have sufficient privileges and your inactive host is registered directly with SCC, you can deregister it by clicking the three dots on its right and selecting De-register.

sect2.title: Identifying inactive systems with &rmt;
sect2.para: By using the rmt-cli systems purge command, you can view and remove hosts that have not contacted the &rmt; server since a given date in the past. For more details, refer to

sect1.title: What system data does SCC collect?
sect1.para: When a system is registered directly via SCC, or its registration information is forwarded by &rmt; or &smlm;, SCC collects the following information:

row.entry: Linux lair 6.9.7-1-default #1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Fri Jun 28 05:50:47 UTC 2024 (a5efffa) x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux
Hardware manufacturer for the system. For virtual machines this is the hypervisor. A sample of the possible values would be Dell Inc., IBM, HP, QEMU, ...
The pacemaker version, if the pacemaker.service is running on the client. For example, 2.1.10+20250718.fdf796ebc8-150700.3.3.1
Depends on the architecture. It includes parameters such as device-tree information or virtualization type on PowerPC or System Z.

entry.para: system_id (string) and instance_types (list of strings) for each SAP installation.
role (string), type (string), and version (string) when a recognised Kubernetes provider is detected.

row.entry: 2021-05-05 (the last time that the system contacted SCC or &rmt;)

entry.para: Base product and activated extensions and modules, for example:

row.entry: The subscription registration code used to activate each product

tip.para: &smlm; sends additional data about the used hypervisor and virtualized systems. Find more details in .

sect2.title: Configuring system data collectors
sect2.para: &suseconnect; collects system data through a configurable collector system. Most collectors are mandatory and cannot be disabled, as they provide essential system information required for proper subscription management and support. However, certain optional collectors can be configured to suit your privacy and performance requirements.

sect3.title: Mandatory collectors
sect3.para: The following collectors are always enabled and cannot be disabled:

sect3.title: Optional collectors
sect3.para: The following collectors are enabled by default but can be disabled through configuration:

sect4.title: Collector configuration
sect4.para: To configure optional collectors, edit the &suseconnect; configuration file at /etc/SUSEConnect. Add a collectors section with the desired collector configuration:
sect4.screen:
--- url: https://scc.suse.com language: en insecure: false auto_agree_with_licenses: false enable_system_uptime_tracking: false no_zypper_refs: false
collectors: pci_data: state: disabled mod_list: state: disabled
sect4.para: For each optional collector, set the state field to one of the following:

note.para: If a collector is not configured in the file, it will use its default state. When a collector is disabled, &suseconnect; will not collect or relay that data when contacting SCC or &rmt;.

sect1.title: What are the benefits of using the keep-alive timer?
sect1.para: The new functionality of &suseconnect; brings the following benefits to the customer:

listitem.para: Ability to identify all types of deployments of systems, no matter if they are derived from a custom image or are clones of an already registered virtual machine.
By contacting SCC or &rmt; regularly, you can obtain the number of actually running registered systems. This provides a better insight into the consumption of &suse; subscriptions.
Updates to registration tools provide a clearer picture of your workloads. You can filter out the systems that are no longer running or decommissioned.
By collecting regular registration and hardware information, we can continue to improve our products to reflect your needs and let you manage system subscriptions more easily.
